articles.title: Now what?
articles.summary: We're living in interesting times where complex projects are being implemented by individuals with impressive technical skills. This is evident in recent examples such as Apple Lisa emulators or modern implementations of long-forgotten languages. As developers, we often focus on the excitement of seeing a working result without considering whether it will be used effectively to solve problems or facilitate learning. The reverse-engineered documentation project Daniel Janus created is an example of this, providing a comprehensive guide to how a calculator's operating system works. By creating something that can be used and appreciated by others, we must consider the ethical implications of our actions and strive for conscious purpose in our development endeavors.

articles.title: Cleaning up after AI rockstar developers
articles.summary: Cleaning up after AI rockstar developers Photo by Martijn Baudoin on Unsplash We've all worked with a rockstar developer who joined years ago, full of energy and great ideas about new tech and architectures. Their cutting-edge ideas left everyone else feeling behind and outdated as they rewrote the company's core architecture and introduced new build processes. The hardest tasks were assigned to them, and their code was often incomprehensible, leading to a culture where nobody understood it but no one would admit it. After they left, teams struggled to keep up with their projects, dealing with messy codebases that required weeks to fix and sometimes even years to rewrite.
